videos.transcript: toronto capital of ontario is the largest city in canada and also one of the most diverse it's home to a dynamic mix of tourist attractions from museums and galleries to the world famous cn tower and just a short drive away is niagara falls you'll find no shortage of things to do from the vibrant entertainment district featuring the latest musicals and fine dining to the historic distillery district home to unique shops and restaurants set in incredibly restored buildings toronto city centre is relatively easy to navigate with many of the top attractions within walking distance of each other and a subway system to cover longer distances if you are visiting toronto in winter head indoors to explore the extensive path network of underground walkways that connect shopping entertainment and attractions in summer wander along the beautiful waterfront and enjoy the beaches and parks for a complete look at how to spend your time see our list of top tourist attractions in toronto in this video we're going to be taking a look at the top 10 places to visit in toronto and just wait until you see the number one that we're going to be showing in this video something you would never even have thought of so make sure you watch till the end oh before we begin you can help support our channel by becoming a member of this channel press the join button below this will help us to bring you more awesome travel videos now let's cut to the chase number 10. city hall and nathan phillips square dominating the spacious nathan phillips square with its bronze sculpture the archer by henry moore is the still highly acclaimed new city hall it was designed by the gifted finnish architect viljo revell and built in 1965. city hall consists of two arc-shaped high-rise blocks 20 and 27 stories high respectively wrapped around a lower central building topped by a flattened cupula in the square in front of city hall is a man-made pond which becomes a popular skating rink in winter and is where the often photographed toronto sign is located this is a beautiful area to visit in december particularly at night when it's decorated for the christmas holidays at nine wander through st lawrence market the saint lawrence market houses a variety of vendors selling various food products flowers and specialty items the saint lawrence hall was built in toronto in 1850 and served as a public meeting place and a concert venue if you've been shopping or touring nearby this is an excellent spot to stop in for a bite to eat or to relax with friends over a cup of coffee in the summer outdoor patios on elevated spaces allow you to soak up some of the warm sunshine the hall was restored in 1967 but has retained much of its old charm the building provides a unique atmosphere for the market and is also occasionally used for film and television shoots the interior features a grand staircase and a gas-lit chandelier uh once you leave st lawrence market head north away from the lake up to front street and then head west for a block and a half to check out the architecturally stunning guderian building at church street this building framed in front of the towers of downtown toronto is an iconic picture of the city at eight tour casa loma standing in beautifully kept grounds casa loma is an extraordinary building somewhat reminiscent of a medieval castle it was originally constructed in 1914 for sir henry pellat an eccentric canadian multi-millionaire who was among the first to recognize and exploit the money-making potential of niagara falls with close to 100 rooms including three dozen bathrooms the house is now a museum shouldn't be too hard to find a restroom then visitors can take a look back in time to a period of european elegance and splendor canada's foremost castle is complete with decorated suites secret passages an 800 foot tunnel towers stables and five acres of estate gardens and a whole lot of lose at seven see the animals at the toronto zoo toronto zoo has an outstanding and diverse collection with approximately five thousand animals some of the favorites include pygmy hippos lions tigers giraffes penguins orangutans and many more the zoo is divided into several sections each representing a major region of the globe some of the other highlights at the toronto zoo include the guerrilla rainforest the tundra trek featuring polar bears and the great barrier reef the discovery zone is a popular area with families and during the summer months a splash pad offers fun in the sun the zoo lies on the rouge river about 40 kilometers north east of the city centre next up at six catch a show or dine in the entertainment district the entertainment district in toronto wonder how they got that name covers a large area of the city centre and includes many of the city's top attractions like the cn tower scotia bank arena rogers center and many museums it's also known for fantastic dining and most importantly shows and performances most of the action is centered around king street between spadina avenue and university avenue toronto's answer to new york's broadway the entertainment district comes to life in the evenings and is a great place for nightlife this is the place to see major theatre productions including musicals concerts and other performing arts some of the most prominent venues in this area are roy thompson hall home to the toronto symphony orchestra and the royal alexandra theatre which hosts the most current musicals at five a day trip to niagara falls if you have never been a quick day trip from toronto to niagara falls is well worth the time and you thought this would be number one you can be standing on the edge of the falls in just over an hour but don't fall in a tour to niagara falls from toronto is an easy way to see the falls if you don't want to drive yourself tours offer hotel pickup and drop-off and include a hornblower niagara cruise which takes you up close to the wall of water tumbling that is the main horseshoe falls tours also stop at some of the key sites in the area including whirlpool rapids the floral clock and the beautiful little town of niagara on the lake a train go train also runs to niagara falls from union station in summer from late june to the start of september as well as the thanksgiving weekend in october on weekends you can even take your bike on the train and enjoy a bike ride along the niagara parkway if you have enough time you may also want to consider spending a night at niagara falls to explore the downtown and see the falls lit up at night next in at 4 art gallery of ontario the renowned art gallery of ontario or ago is one of the largest museums in north america the collection of more than 95 000 pieces includes works from around the world from european masterpieces to contemporary art but also holds an impressive collection of canadian art including a vast collection of works by the group of seven a series of temporary exhibitions are mounted throughout the year the ago occupies a unique looking building with a mix of older and modern architecture on the west side of the city centre next to the ago is the heart to miss ontario college of art and design standing high above the street on stilts designed to look like pencils no not pencils exactly at number three ripley's aquarium of canada one of toronto's newest top attractions is the ripley's aquarium near the base of the cn tower this fabulous facility displays all kinds of marine life and is one of the most popular things to do in toronto four families the most impressive feature is the huge underwater tunnel with a moving sidewalk you can watch the ocean world go by all around you as sharks glide past and sawfish linger on the tunnel roof above this is a truly serene experience for all ages unless you don't like sharks another unexpected highlight is the jellyfish display accented with creative lighting further on touch tanks with stingrays and small sharks allow for a hands-on experience and for the engineering minded the building's open concept also allows for a look at the life support system and filtration equipment operating the facility at number two visit the royal ontario museum the royal ontario museum known as the rom is one of canada's premier museums with an international reputation for excellence it houses an outstanding collection which covers natural history art and culture from a great variety of periods from all over the world it is also well known for featuring exhibitions from across the globe a controversial expansion in 2007 saw the addition of the michael lee chin crystal a modern wing featuring glass and sharp angles added on to a very traditional older building it's now one of toronto's most recognizable buildings and finally at number one it's not niagara falls see the view from the cn tower toronto's famous landmark the 553 metre cn tower is one of the city's must-see attractions and also the most impossible to miss towering above the downtown this canadian icon can be seen from almost everywhere in the city you have the option of simply appreciating the building from the ground or taking a trip up to one of the observation areas or restaurants for fabulous views over the city and lake ontario the cn tower built between 1972 and 1976 was once the tallest freestanding structure in the world but has long since been surpassed the highest viewing area on the cn tower is from the sky pod at 447 metres above the city with views that on clear days extend to niagara falls and new york state to get here requires taking two elevators below this at the top of the main elevator is the lookout level at 346 meters with floor-to-ceiling windows and the new glass floor which looks down to the original glass floor one floor below where the outdoor sky terrace is located as the name suggests the glass floor offers a bird's eye view directly down over the city no thanks for those looking for a little more adventure though or perhaps a lot more adventure there is the edge walk this involves a hands-free walk on a 1.5 meter wide ledge around the outside edge of the main pod at an elevation of 365 meters participants are attached to a safety harness and rope really located at 351 metres is the revolving 360 restaurant featuring fine dining and some of the best views from a table anywhere in toronto and without risking your life 360 is open for lunch and dinner and visitors who dine here also receive complimentary access to the lookout and glass floor levels of the tower still no thanks basing yourself in the city centre preferably near the cn tower is the best option for exploring toronto and there you have the top 10 things to do in toronto did you like what you saw let us know in the comments down below share this video with your friends and don't forget to subscribe to our channel for more fantastic travel guides see you next time height lovers

videos.description:
Toronto Travel Guide 2025 - Best Places to Visit in Toronto Canada in 2025

Toronto, the capital of the province of Ontario, is a major Canadian city along Lake Ontario’s northwestern shore. It's a dynamic metropolis with a core of soaring skyscrapers, all dwarfed by the iconic, free-standing CN Tower. Toronto also has many green spaces, from the orderly oval of Queen’s Park to 400-acre High Park and its trails, sports facilities and zoo. 

Canada is a country in North America. Its ten provinces and three territories extend from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean and northward into the Arctic Ocean, making it the world's second-largest country by total area, with the world's longest coastline.

videos.transcript: [Music] Toronto, a vibrant multicultural metropolis where towering skyscrapers meet historic charm. From its iconic skyline to the diverse neighborhoods pulsing with culture, this city offers something for every traveler. Whether you're into art, food, nature, or nightife, Toronto delivers an experience that's both global and uniquely Canadian. In this video, we'll explore the sights, sounds, and stories that make this city unforgettable. Get ready to discover the true essence of Toronto beyond the postcards and into the heart of one of North America's most dynamic cities. [Music] Rising over 550 m, the CN Tower dominates Toronto skyline and stands as a symbol of Canadian engineering. Once the tallest freestanding structure in the world, it offers breathtaking views stretching across Lake Ontario and the city below. The glass floor and sky pod take the thrill to new heights, while Edgewalk lets the daring step outside, literally. Whether you're here for the view, the adrenaline, or a meal in the 360°ree restaurant, the CN Tower is an unforgettable stop that captures the scale and spirit of Toronto. [Music] Lending striking modern architecture with classic heritage, the Royal Ontario Museum is a gateway to the world's natural and cultural wonders. Inside its vast collections span everything from ancient civilizations and indigenous artifacts to towering dinosaur skeletons and rare minerals. Each exhibit invites curiosity, making it a place where history, science, and art come alive. Whether you're a casual visitor or a lifelong learner, the ROM offers a journey through time and across continents right in the heart of Toronto. Located at the base of the CN Tower, Ripley's Aquarium of Canada plunges you into an underwater world full of color and motion. Home to over 20,000 aquatic animals, it features mesmerizing exhibits like the dangerous lagoon, where a moving walkway takes you through a tunnel surrounded by sharks, stingrays, and sea turtles. From vibrant jellyfish to hands-on touch tanks, every corner invites you to explore the mysteries of the deep. It's a captivating experience for visitors of all ages right in downtown Toronto. Just a short ferry ride from the city, the Toronto Islands offer a peaceful escape from the urban buzz. With car-free streets, scenic beaches, and lush parklands, it's the perfect spot for biking, picnicking, or simply unwinding by the water. The views of the skyline from here are some of the best in the city. Each island has its own charm. From Center Island's familyfriendly attractions to Wards Island's cozy cottages and quiet trails, it's a refreshing dose of nature right on Toronto's doorstep. Cobblestone streets, Victorian era industrial buildings, and a creative spirit define the distillery historic district. Once the largest distillery in the British Empire, this area has been transformed into a vibrant hub for art, culture, and cuisine. Boutique shops, galleries, and cafes fill the preserved red brick warehouses, while outdoor installations and seasonal festivals keep the atmosphere lively year round. It's a place where history meets modern flare, offering a unique glimpse into Toronto's past and present. A cornerstone of Toronto's culinary scene, St. Lawrence Market is a feast for the senses. With roots dating back to the early 1800s, it's home to dozens of vendors offering everything from fresh produce and artisal cheeses to international street food and local delicacies. The energy here is warm and welcoming with generations of familyrun stalls and stories behind every counter. Whether you're grabbing a famous peal bacon sandwich or browsing handmade goods, the market captures the city's flavor in every way. [Music] Perched on a hill overlooking the city, Kasaloma feels like stepping into a European fairy tale. This Gothic revival mansion built in the early 1900s features grand halls, secret passages, and sweeping staircases that tell the story of a dreamer with big ambitions. Wander through opulent rooms, lush gardens, and even underground tunnels. With its rich history and dramatic architecture, Kasaloma offers a glimpse into a different era and one of Toronto's most enchanting landmarks. [Music] Eclectic, colorful, and full of character, Kensington Market is one of Toronto's most vibrant neighborhoods. Here, street art meets vintage shops, global eats, and an unmistakable bohemian vibe. Every corner feels alive with creativity. From reggae beats to handpainted signs and diverse food stalls offering flavors from around the world, it's a place where cultures blend and individuality thrives, making it one of the most authentic expressions of Toronto's multicultural identity. [Music] as the civic heart of Toronto, Nathan Phillips Square is a gathering place for locals and visitors alike. Framed by the modern curves of city hall and the iconic Toronto sign, it's a popular backdrop for photos and public events year round. In summer, the reflecting pool brings a sense of calm to the busy downtown, while in winter, it transforms into a lively skating rink. From festivals to peaceful moments, this square captures the pulse of the city in every season. [Music] Bright lights, buzzing energy, and non-stop motion, Young Dundas Square is Toronto's answer to Time Square. Surrounded by massive screens, shopping centers, and entertainment venues, it's a hub where the city's fast-paced spirit comes alive. Street performers, pop-up events, and live concerts keep the atmosphere dynamic day and night. Whether you're passing through or soaking it all in, this intersection is where Toronto shows off its bold, modern side. [Music]
